items.front: Where may Special VFR operations be authorized?
items.back: Only within the lateral boundaries of Class B, C, D, or E surface areas below 10,000 feet MSL, unless prohibited or specially exempted. Class E extensions are not authorized for SVFR.

items.front: What priority rule applies between SVFR and IFR traffic?
items.back: Approve SVFR only if it will not delay arriving or departing IFR aircraft. If delay is expected, tell the pilot the anticipated delay.

items.front: What separation methods may be used for SVFR fixed-wing aircraft?
items.back: Use nonradar or visual separation. Radar separation is not authorized for SVFR fixed-wing operations, though radar vectors may be used to expedite entry, exit, or transition.

items.front: How are altitudes assigned when vertical separation is used for SVFR?
items.back: Do not assign a fixed altitude. Clear the aircraft at or below an altitude at least 500 feet below conflicting IFR traffic and not below the 91.119 minimum safe altitude.

items.front: When may local SVFR operations be authorized?
items.back: On request, for a specified period, if the aircraft can be recalled when traffic or weather conditions require.

items.front: When may an aircraft be authorized to climb to VFR?
items.back: On pilot request, if restricted visibility is the only weather limitation.

items.front: What happens to SVFR fixed-wing requests when official ground visibility is below 1 mile?
items.back: Departures cannot be cleared. Arrivals outside the surface area cannot be cleared unless an emergency exists. Arrivals inside the surface area are told the visibility and asked for intentions.

items.front: What happens when weather is not reported and an SVFR fixed-wing pilot says flight visibility is below 1 mile?
items.back: Departures cannot be cleared, arrivals outside the surface area cannot be cleared unless an emergency exists, and arrivals inside the surface area are asked for intentions.
